findings.description: The HTML table splits the header structure differently from the PDF. The PDF has a compact multi-line header with Report #, Lab, PQL, and Units columns clearly grouped, with sample dates and AC numbers organized in merged column headers. The HTML table has an expanded, less compact header that separates these differently.
findings.evidence: PDF shows a dense table header with 'Report #', 'Lab PQL', 'Units\Hg 1631' in the left columns and date-based column groups (2/14/2014, 2/17/2014, 2/26/2014, 2/27/2014) each containing AC numbers and Value/Qualifier sub-columns. HTML renders a wider header with similar information but the column grouping and alignment differs noticeably.

findings.description: Some qualifier 'U' flags appear in separate columns in the PDF but the HTML table structure handles these slightly differently, though the data is present.
findings.evidence: PDF shows U qualifiers in dedicated narrow qualifier columns next to values; HTML preserves the qualifier columns but the visual association between value and qualifier is slightly different due to column width differences.

findings.description: The HTML renders the table with significantly more vertical spacing, making the single-page PDF content extend much further vertically. The compact, dense layout of the PDF is lost, reducing the ability to scan and compare data across rows.
findings.evidence: PDF fits all data including footnotes on a single page with a dense table. HTML spreads the same content over a much longer vertical scroll, with each row taking substantially more vertical space.

findings.description: The 'Applicable Water Quality Standard' and 'Basis for Standard' columns in the HTML appear to have truncated or wrapped text differently than the PDF.
findings.evidence: PDF shows 'no NC standards for dissolved metals' clearly in the Applicable column for Al-Dissolved Aluminum. HTML shows 'no NC standards for' and 'no RC standards for' in separate cells with slightly different text wrapping.

findings.description: The HTML fabricates content in the 'Applicable Water Quality Standard' and 'Basis for Standard' columns for the dissolved metals section. The PDF shows 'no NC standards for dissolved metals' as a single note spanning the standard/basis columns for dissolved metals, but the HTML repeats this text across two separate cells for both 'Al - Dissolved Aluminum' and 'As Dissolved Arsenic' rows, creating fabricated cell content.
findings.evidence: PDF: 'no NC standards for dissolved metals' appears once as a note. HTML: Both 'Al - Dissolved Aluminum' and 'As Dissolved Arsenic' rows have 'no NC standards for' and 'dissolved metals' in both their Standard and Basis columns.

findings.description: The empty separator row between the total metals section and dissolved metals section is included in the HTML as an entirely blank row, which is a minor structural artifact, but more importantly several column header details are muddled. The header structure conflates Report #, Lab, Dissolved, PQL, and Units/Hg 1631 sub-headers into confusing merged column names.
findings.evidence: PDF has distinct sub-header rows: 'Report #' with AC04986, 'Lab Dissolved' with AC04987, 'PQL Units\Hg 1631' with AC04993. HTML merges these into 'Report # Dissolved Units\Hg 1631' as a single column header.

findings.description: The document uses h1, h2, and h3 for the title, subtitle, and sub-subtitle. The PDF doesn't have a clear hierarchical heading structure - it's more of a report header block. Using h3 for 'Sampling Dates and Sample ID' is acceptable but debatable since it's a table descriptor, not a true section heading.
findings.evidence: HTML: h1='Data from Ash Release at Duke Energy Dan River Station', h2='Stormwater Discharge Upstream of Ash Pond - 009', h3='Sampling Dates and Sample ID'

findings.description: The blank/separator row between total metals and dissolved metals is rendered as a full empty row in the HTML table. This is a minor structural artifact but doesn't cause content duplication.
findings.evidence: HTML contains a with 13 empty elements between Zinc_Zn and Al - Dissolved Aluminum rows.

findings.description: Minor text normalization: underscores in parameter names are replaced with spaces throughout (e.g., 'Residue_Suspended' becomes 'Residue Suspended', 'Aluminum_Al' becomes 'Aluminum Al'). This is a consistent reformatting that doesn't change meaning.
findings.evidence: PDF: 'Residue_Suspended', 'Aluminum_Al', 'Arsenic_As', etc. HTML: 'Residue Suspended', 'Aluminum Al', 'Arsenic As', etc.
